## [00:00 - 03:05]

Okay, let's have a conversation around bringing clients into your business, whether you're freelancing or you're starting to build out a production team around you. This process is pretty similar with a few edits along the way, so I'm just going to type it out as we go. So step number one always starts with send contract and invoice. Now a little bit of a tip here and you'll see an example below this video. When you send a contract, here's two ways that you can pretty much go about it. You can use software tools like Proposify or DoveSato. There's lots and lots of tools out there. And once the client signs a contract, it automates where it will automatically send them to a payment portal for the invoice amount. This significantly helps in reducing the amount of people that ghost you, essentially, that will say yes and sign contracts and then you're chasing up the invoice for ages. If someone wasn't intending on working with you, though, it's not going to help with that. So this is really just to help make that whole process as seamless as possible and reduce any friction. So you're not having to increase your own workload and chase up invoices when really that's just a bit of a waste of time for yourself and for the client. The second way you can do this that some agencies or freelancers do is they will send the contract but there will be nowhere to sign, nowhere to place a signature on the contract. And at the end of the contract, there's a stipulation that says this contract will be deemed as accepted by the client upon payment to the nominated bank account and you follow that up and send an invoice attached with that. So it's basically saying if you accept these contract terms, we're going to deem that as acceptance once we receive payment. Now the key for that is you still want a signature. So after the client has made payment, you could either trigger an automated contract and then send it to them where it asks them for a signature or just say, hey, we just need to also have your signature on our files while we're here. Those are two ways that you can pretty much ask for payment for the client but nevertheless, the point of this is you should always start with this. Now some freelancers and agencies will be asking for a percentage of the sum or the total sum whereas some will be asking for the entire amount upfront. So for example, if you're pitching $10,000 for a website build, some freelancers are going to charge 30% upfront, 30% in the middle and then 30% upon completion whereas some

## [03:05 - 06:11]

are just going to charge 10K upfront. I would say if you're just getting started out and you're just cutting your teeth on the whole sales process and you don't quite have a team or an agency, there's a little bit less of a risk needed to protect yourself because when you think about it, when you're an agency, you have staff costs, you have really high overheads. So it's really important that you protect yourself and your cash flow whereas in this situation, if you're doing most of the work, you're not really outsourcing or tapping into team members, it's safe enough usually to break that apart into payment installments such as 30%. It can be a personal choice as well and just what you are most comfortable with. I would always recommend charging upfront because it's always going to be the safest way to do it but of course there might be some clients that that is not feasible for and also it might be a situation where you don't even need to do that because you don't have those kinds of production costs. It's not as front loaded depending on the service that you're offering. So the second thing that we're going to do and we can actually do this before we've received payment but we're going to create client folder and I do this in Drive but you can do this in any platform that you're using really. So basically what I do is I just go in Google Drive and I create under the agency client folders, I create a folder for this client and inside of that I'm basically going to be putting things like the briefing document that they're going to complete for me. I'll talk to you guys about that in a second and then client assets and game plans. Finally I would create a separate folder within this folder for their assets and so they can't see everything else that we have about them and they can only really see like one folder which usually is just the photos or whatever or the scoping scope of work document. Third step is we're then going to send onboarding email and this is basically a welcome email, welcome to the family fold, super excited to be working with you and to helping you, you know, whatever solution it is that they're tapping into. In here you want to link to the briefing document. Now the briefing document is basically just a Google document. I'm sharing an example with you below that you can check out. Bear in mind the example I share with you is for Facebook media buying services on a specific niche so please do not duplicate that briefing document. You need to customize this and what you're wanting to do is what are the questions that you need answered in order for what is the information that you need in order to implement the service

## [06:11 - 09:13]

that the client is paying for. What do you need from them? What do you need to know? Do you need to know their targeting, their personas, their goals, their Facebook pages, all of those things. So asking those questions from them. And so the briefing document is usually just a Google document and in your onboarding email, welcome to the team. When you have a minute, can you please start filling out this briefing document? Now if you're finding that you're having to chase clients up a lot and they're not filling this out fully or they're taking a lot of time, a little bit of a tip here, excuse me, that was shared by Katia is to have a penalty fee for late submission. So explaining to the client, hey, you have one week to fill out this briefing document and if it's not completed in a week, there will be a penalty fee and we will need to reassess your spot on our production teams. Normally it means that everyone's going to fill it out right away because no one likes penalty fees or to be knocked off of priority lists. Okay, the next thing we're going to do is this is triggered only once the contract is signed and paid. Contract invoice is we're going to reassess the deliverables based off the briefing document completed. We're going to agree to resources and to the timeline and basically scope out the project. I should have put that there, scope out project. There we go. So we're looking at what do I need to do? What's the timeline? Do I need to pull in any resources for this? Hopefully this is a process that you already have in your business and you already know this stuff. And then finally we're going to have a kickoff call with the client and this is just resetting expectations, whatever those might be for whatever service you're offering, briefing document. So if they going through it making sure that they filled it out properly, if there's any missing pieces, etc, etc. And then you're basically communicating with them, you know, the timeline, how much ad spend they're going to need, anything that's relevant during this call, right? And then lastly we're going to kick off the project. So start scope, you know, roll out the scope of the project. So those are the six steps to onboarding clients into your business as smoothly as possible. There's some examples and a few other tips below this video.

## [09:14 - 09:28]

Hopefully this helps. If you can smooth out this process, you can pretty much roll this out pretty efficiently in your business and make it an experience that your clients will come to appreciate as well as part of working with you.
